title: Container cleaning rotation
summary: A rotation for inspecting, cleaning, drying, labeling, and retiring water containers before contamination or mixed uses create risk.
content: Stored water is only as safe as the container. A container that held fuel, cleaners, unknown liquids, or dirty runoff should not become drinking-water storage. Every container needs a role, last-cleaned date, inspection status, and separate storage for caps.
steps: Reject unknown chemical containers for drinking water.
Inspect caps, cracks, smell, slime, and corners.
Clean and dry before refilling according to safe guidance.
Retire damaged containers to non-drinking use or discard.
warnings: Do not use disinfectants by guesswork.
Do not drink from containers that smell of fuel or solvent.
Do not dip dirty cups into stored water.
common_mistakes: All containers look the same with no labels.
Refilling before inspection because people rush.
Drying clean containers where animals or dust reach them.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Container cleaning rotation with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Container cleaning rotation if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Container cleaning rotation and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Container cleaning rotation or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Container cleaning rotation are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Container cleaning rotation”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Container cleaning rotation as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Container cleaning rotation when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Container cleaning rotation.
short_term: For the first hours of Container cleaning rotation, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Container cleaning rotation remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Container cleaning rotation fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Container cleaning rotation to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
